coredata.dc:title: N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots as nanoprobe for detection of tartrazine in beverages
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xiupei
Xu, Jing
Luo, Na
Tang, Fenglin
Zhang, Maoxue
Zhao, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Chemical doping with heteroatoms was employed to prepare N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,Cl-FCDs) which may be designed for the detection of tartrazine. Using urea as the N source and FeCl3 · 6H2O as the Cl source, N,Cl-FCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with carbon dots prepared from aloe. Compared with pure carbon dots (CDs), N,Cl-FCDs resulted in dramatic improvement in the fluorescence properties and surface physical chemical properties. The fluorescence quantum yield of the N,Cl-FCDs was as high as 60.52%. As an effective fluorescence probe for tartrazine, a good linear relationship between N,Cl-FCDs and tartrazine was constructed at the concentration range from 0.1 to 30 μM with a detection limit of 48 nM based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The proposed fluorescent probe method was successfully applied to detect tartrazine in beverages, which is expected to have a potential application in the field of food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots
Tartrazine
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM (A); the XPS spectra (B), S 2p spectra (C), C 1s spectra (D), N 1s spectra (E) and O 1s spectra (F) of N,Cl-FCDs.
UV–vis absorption (left) and fluorescence emission (right) spectra of the N,Cl-FCDs and CDs-1.
UV–vis absorption spectra of tartrazine, N,Cl-FCDs and tartrazine + N,Cl-FCDs (A); Overlapping between fluorescence spetra of N,Cl-FCDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of tartrazine (B).

The fluorescence spectra of N,Cl-FCDs upon addition different concentration of tartrazine, the inset pattern is the linear calibration plot for tartrazine (A); Interference test of the system of N,Cl-FCDs and tartrazine, phosphate buffer pH = 5, the concentration of tartrazine was 20 µM, the K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, HCO3 −, Cl−, glucose, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, citric acid, lactose, boric acid were100 times that of tartrazine, rhodamine B, methylene blue, malachite Green were 10 times that of tartrazine. Sunset yellow and bright blue were 1 time that of tartrazine (B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Scheme of the synthetic strategy for N,Cl-FCDs and the principle of tartrazine sensing.
Comparison of the proposed method with other methods for determination of tartrazine.

row.entry: (Celina Selvakumari, Nishanthi, Dhanalakshmi, Ahila, & Pathinettam Padiyan, 2018)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test and precision of the analysis of tartrazine in real beverage samples.

head.ce:title: N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots as nanoprobe for detection of tartrazine in beverages

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Material Chemistry, MOE, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering Tianjin, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical doping with heteroatoms was employed to prepare N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,Cl-FCDs) which may be designed for the detection of tartrazine. Using urea as the N source and FeCl3 · 6H2O as the Cl source, N,Cl-FCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with carbon dots prepared from aloe. Compared with pure carbon dots (CDs), N,Cl-FCDs resulted in dramatic improvement in the fluorescence properties and surface physical chemical properties. The fluorescence quantum yield of the N,Cl-FCDs was as high as 60.52%. As an effective fluorescence probe for tartrazine, a good linear relationship between N,Cl-FCDs and tartrazine was constructed at the concentration range from 0.1 to 30 μM with a detection limit of 48 nM based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The proposed fluorescent probe method was successfully applied to detect tartrazine in beverages, which is expected to have a potential application in the field of food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Tartrazine, also known as lemon yellow, is a water-soluble synthetic pigment widely used as a food additive in certain foods such as sweets, beverages and jellies (Wang & Zhao, 2015). However, some studies have shown that people who consume a large amount of foods with excessive pigments such as tartrazine may suffer from diarrhea, allergies and if excessive intake, kidneys and liver can be possibly damaged (Sakthivel, Sivakumar, Chen, & Pandi, 2018). Therefore, it is necessary to establish a simple, efficient, and economical method for the detection of tartrazine in food products such as beverages.

Fluorescence carbon dots (FCDs) are a new kind of carbon nanomaterials, which have the advantages of chemical inertness, biocompatibility, good water solubility, low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, rich surface groups, and easy functionalization (Liu et al., 2016). Considerable methods have been developed to fabricate CDs, including chemical and electrochemical oxidation (Bao et al., 2011), electrochemical exfoliation (Lu et al., 2009), microwave method (Hou et al., 2018), hydrothermal method (Yang et al., 2011), carbonizing organic molecules (Tang et al., 2012). Hydrothermal method is one of the most popular methods because of its simplicity and economy (Xu, Yang, Li, Zhao, & Liao, 2015). Natural products such as sweet pepper (Yin et al., 2013), petal (Feng, Zhong, Miao, & Yang, 2015), silk (Li et al., 2013), gardenia (Yang et al., 2018), and orange juice (Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012) could be used as carbon source to prepare fluorescence CDs. Although economical in raw materials and practical in methods for preparation, the quantum yield of the synthesis of CDs from natural products is still low and its fluorescence property is not desirable. Therefore, it is necessary to further modify the fluorescence properties of this kind of CDs.

According to semiconductor theory, doping heterogeneous atoms is an effective way to adjust the band gap of semiconductor. Therefore, the intrinsic properties of the CDs could be changed by altering the internal electronic structure through doping with different elements, which increase the fluorescence quantum yield (Gong & Zhao, 2018; Hu et al., 2019; Mashkani, Mehdinia, Jabbari, Bide, & Nabid, 2018). In this paper, the fluorescence properties of N,Cl co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,Cl-FCDS) prepared by natural products were adjusted by a simple hydrothermal method (Luo et al., 2018). The N,Cl-FCDs exhibited sufficient salt and pH tolerance and excellent fluorescence properties. Based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between tartrazine and N,Cl-FCDs, a rapid, convenient, and sensitive test method for tartrazine was designed. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was proportional to the concentration of tartrazine with a linear range of 0.1–30 μM and the detection limit of 48 nM. The proposed method was further applied to the determination of tartrazine in beverages with good recovery and precision.

Fresh aloe was collected from potted plants grown in our laboratory and washed with ultrapure water for further use. FeCl3 · 6H2O, Na2HPO4 · 12H2O, NaOH, Rhodamine 6G, rhodamine B, tartrazine, sunset yellow, brilliant blue and ascorbic acid were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corp. (Shanghai, China). Urea, HCl (36.0–38.0%), malachite green and lactose were received from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals (Chengdu, China). NaCl, NaHCO3, citric acid, boric acid and glucose were obtained from Chongqing Maoye Chemicals (Chongqing, China). NaH2PO4 · 2H2O, methylene blue and tartaric acid were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Deionized water with 18.2 MΩ·cm from UPH-II-20 T water purification system (Chengdu Ultrapure Technology Co. Ltd., Chengdu, China) was used throughout this work.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were taken on a TECNAI G2 F20 (FEI, USA) operating at 200 KV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) with KBr pellets at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements were conducted with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA). All absorption spectra were detected by a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan).

A hydrothermal method was used to prepare the N,Cl-FCDs. 5 g of shredded fresh aloe and 25 mL of ultrapure water were mixed and then transferred into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Weihai Huixin Chemical Machinery Co., Ltd., Weihai, China) followed by heating at 180 °C for 11 h. After the reaction, the resulting mixture was filtrated by 0.22 μm membrane (Beihua Liming Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) and the filtrates were collected as CDs solution. FeCl3 · 6H2O (1.3 g) and urea (0.5 g) were mixed with 24 mL of CDs solution followed by transferring into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heating at 180 °C for 36 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the resulting mixture was centrifuged in a TG16A-WS high speed centrifuge (Luxiangyi Centrifuge Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) for 15 min at 12000 rpm, and the N,Cl-FCDs aqueous solution was collected by decantation and stored at 4 °C.

The calculation of quantum yield of CDs referred to the previously reported method (Dai et al., 2014). Briefly, Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) aqueous solution (φ = 95%) was used as a reference standard, and the ultraviolet absorption and integrated fluorescence intensity of Rh 6G and N,Cl-FCDs were measured at the same conditions. The quantum yield was calculated according to the equation (Yang et al., 2018):
(1)
φ χ
=
φ
st
I χ
A χ
A
st
I
st
η χ
η
st
2
where φ, I, A, η are the quantum yield, the corrected emission intensity at the excitation wavelength, the absorbance, the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts “st” and “x” represent reference standards and samples.

The distance between the acceptor and the donor (r), the distance between Föster (R0) and the energy transfer efficiency (E) can be calculated by the following equations:
(2)
J
λ
=
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
ε A
λ
λ 4
d λ
(3)
R 0
= 0.2108
κ 2
× ϕ ×
n
R
- 4
× J
λ
1 6
(4)
E = 1 -
τ D
τ
DA
= 1 /
1 +
r
R 0
6
where τD and τDA are the average fluorescence lifetime of N,Cl-FCDs in the presence and in the absence of tartrazine, K2 is the orientation factor between the donor and acceptor transition dipole, which is usually assumed to be 2/3. The refractive index of medium and the fluorescence quantum yield of donor are represented by n and φ, and the value of n in this article is 1.36. J (λ) is the integral of overlap values, and εA (λ) is the molar absorptivity of the receptor at wavelength λ.

Orange juice and sugar candy sydney were purchased at a local supermarket in Nanchong. Before opening, beverages were shaken to ensure their uniformity and then diluted with water to produce 10-fold dilution. The dilution was sonicated for 10 min followed by filtrating through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and collecting the filtrate. The filtrate was stored at 4 °C.

The detection of tartrazine was performed in 500 μL phosphate buffer solution (pH = 5). Typically, 300 μL of N,Cl-FCDs solutions were first dispersed into 500 μL phosphate buffer solution, and then different concentrations of tartrazine solution were added. The resulting solution was shaken by hand to ensure the mixture was thoroughly mixed before recording the fluorescence emission spectra.

Fig. 1 A showed the TEM images of the as-synthesized N,Cl-FCDs. It revealed that the N,Cl-FCDs were spherical shape with average diameter around 5 nm. HRTEM images of N,Cl-FCDs indicated the interfacial spacing of N,Cl-FCDs with about 0.229 nm, which was close to the (1 0 0) diffraction facets of graphite (Liang, Kang, Payne, Wang, & Sun, 2016). The surface chemical state, composition and bonding of N,Cl-FCDs were investigated by XPS shown in Fig. 1B–F. Four peaks in Fig. 1B at 198.6, 286.6, 400.6, 532.6 eV were assigned to Cl 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The composition analysis of the N,Cl-FCDs showed the following elemental composition: Cl (0.45%), C (55.85%), N (1.14%) and O (42.56%). Although the N and Cl contents in N,Cl-FCDs were relatively, it implied that the N,Cl co-doped CDs with high quantum yield were successfully synthesized. The peaks at 198.1 and 199.8 eV in XPS (Fig. 1C) were observed in the spectrum of Cl 2p (Singh, Raj, Sen, Johari, & Mitra, 2017), further confirming the presence of Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum exhibited the peaks centered at 284.9, 286.6, 288.2 eV, which could be ascribed to CC/CC, CN and CO, respectively (Fig. 1D) (Xu, Bao, Zhou, Zeng, & Hu, 2016). Two main peaks in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum were assigned to aromatic N at 400.0 eV and hydric N at 401.9 eV (Fig. 1E). Moreover, O 1s survey scan, Fig. 1F, showed two peaks at 531.4 and 533.0 eV, corresponding to CO and CO (Dong, Yuan, Zhang, Lin, & Lu, 2017).

The FTIR spectrum was recorded to study various types of functional groups on the N,Cl-FCDs (Fig. S1). Compared with CDs, the absorption of CDs at 3400, 2931 and 1400 cm−1 can be overlapped with the spectrum of N,Cl-FCDs, indicating that the surface of N,Cl-FCDs have the functional groups of OH, CH, COOH, which come from CDs (Xu et al., 2015). The absorption at 1400 cm−1 of N,Cl-FCDs also verified the existence of the vibration of CN bond, foreshadowing the formation of amides (Ding, Wei, & Xiong, 2014). The stretching vibration of NH and OH were located at the broad peak around 3151 cm−1 (Xu et al., 2016). The peak at 1625 cm−1 was attributed to CC bond stretching, which was implied that unsaturated carbon was produced in the reaction (Zhu, Guo, Fang, & Dong, 2010).

All results of XPS and FTIR spectra clearly confirmed that N and Cl atoms had been doped in the CDs. It is inferred that the electronic properties of N,Cl-FCDs in water and surface physicochemical properties may be affected due to the introduction of N and Cl atoms.

Considering that doping is an effective method for adjusting the intrinsic properties of CDs (Qu et al., 2017), the optical properties of CDs can be modified by introducing N and Cl. As shown in Fig. 2 , the pink lines was corresponded to the ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs, respectively. Contrasting to the strong absorption of CDs at 278 nm, two weak absorption peaks of the N,Cl-FCDs were observed at 264 nm and 323 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the double bonds containing groups such as CO and CC, respectively (Zhu et al., 2015). The aqueous solution of N,Cl-FCDs exhibited bright blue luminescence at 365 nm UV light. The blue line was the fluorescence emission spectrum of the N,Cl-FCDs. When compared with the CDs, the fluorescence spectra of the N,Cl-FCDs was approximately blue shift of 60 nm, which may be due to the fact that N-doping can usually induce or enhance blue fluorescence emitted by CDs (Liu, Guo, Rao, Zhang, & Gong, 2013).

The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S2). There was obvious shift in the emission peak with changes in excitation wavelengths from 360 to 460 nm, and the N,Cl-FCDs demonstrated the strong fluorescence emission peak at 441 nm when excited at 323 nm. Using Rh 6G as a reference, the quantum yield for N,Cl-FCDs was calculated to be 60.52% which is six times that of CDs (Xu et al., 2015), which may be ascribed to the structure of amide engendered by N-doping. Amides can effectively enhance the surface state of N,Cl-FCDs (Zhu et al., 2015), and Cl may also has synergistic effect on the fluorescence enhancement of N,Cl-FCDs.

Fluorescence stability is a particularly important property of CDs. The as-prepared N,Cl-FCDs are observed to be stable with the pH at 4–12 (Fig. S3A). Such stability may be due to the change of surface charge caused by the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups of N,Cl-FCDs (Dong et al., 2013). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged even when the NaCl concentration reached 1.0 M (Fig. S3B), which indicated that the synthesized N,Cl-FCDs possessed excellent salt-tolerance.

The fluorescence intensities of N,Cl-FCDs with and without tartrazine were detected and the results were shown in Fig. S4. When a certain concentration of tartrazine was added to N,Cl-FCDs, the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl-FCDs solution decreased, and obvious fluorescence quenching occurs. In order to understand the interaction between tartrazine and N,Cl-FCDs, we investigated the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence quenching includes static and dynamic quenching. We have detected the UV absorption spectrum of N,Cl-FCDs, tartrazine, N,Cl-FCDs-Tartrazine. The results were presented in Fig. 3 A, showing that the UV–vis absorption of tartrazine exhibited two strong absorption peaks at 256 and 429 nm. In addition, there was no new UV–vis absorption peak or blue/red shift for the N,Cl-FCDs-Tartrazine, which revealed that there were no new compounds (Joseph & Anappara, 2016). Hence, the reason for the decrease of PL intensity is not caused by static quenching mechanism.

After further detailed study, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of tartrazine and the fluorescence spectra of N,Cl-FCDs had a large overlap shown in Fig. 3B, which implied that the quenching mechanism might be Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). According to Föster theory of non-radiative energy transfer, the following conditions are required to generate FRET (Liang et al., 2016): ①Effective overlap between the acceptor absorption spectrum and the donor emission spectrum; ②Appropriate orientation between the dipole of acceptor asn donor; ③The distance between the receptor and the donor must be 10–100 Å. On the basis of formula (2)–(4), the results display that J (λ) is 1.99 × 1014 M−1 cm−1 nm4, and R0 is 35.6 Å, which is between 10 and 100 Å. Therefore, it was suggested that the interaction between tartrazine and N,Cl-FCDs occurred, and it was very likely that tartrazine, as an energy acceptor, and N,Cl-FCDs act as an energy donor to generate fluorescence resonance energy transfer, which leads to fluorescence quenching. The synthetic strategy of N,Cl-FCDs and the principle of tartrazine sensing were schematically represented in Scheme 1 .

In order to get a more sensitive signal for determination of tartrazine, the optimized detection condition such as pH, the dosage of N,Cl-FCDs and reaction temperature were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5A, the effect of the solution pH on the fluorescence intensity was investigated in the range of 4.5–6, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl-FCDs in the absence and presence of tartrazine) increased with the rise of pH firstly, and reached the maximum at the pH 5, whereas a further increase in pH from 5 to 6 led to a gradual decrease in F0/F. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the solution of N,Cl-FCDs was very stable at pH 4–12. Such an observation suggested that the solution of pH may impact the surface state of tartrazine, thus affecting the interaction between N,Cl-FCDs and tartrazine. Therefore, buffer solution at pH = 5 was selected for the following experiments.

The effect of the dosage of N,Cl-FCDs on the quantitative detection of tartrazine was exhibited in Fig. S5B. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the system increased gradually with an increase in the dosage of N,Cl-FCDs from 100 to 300 μL. When the dosage of N,Cl-FCDs was greater than 300 μL, the quenching efficiency decreased. Consequently, a dosage of 300 μL was chosen as an optimum condition.

The effects of the reaction temperature on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of this system were investigated at 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 °C. As shown in Fig. S5C, the quenching efficiency increased with an increase in reaction temperature from 0 to 5 °C, nevertheless a further increase in temperature from 5 to 25 °C led to an evidently decrease in F0/F. The experimental results indicated that the low temperature was more favorable for the reaction, and 5 °C was used for subsequent experiments.

Under the optimum reaction conditions, different concentrations of tartrazine were added to the system in order to determine the change of fluorescence intensity, which was shown in Fig. 4 A. It displayed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,Cl-FCDs decrease gradually with the increase of the concentration of tartrazine. The standard curve was exhibited in the insert of Fig. 4, the fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of tartrazine in the detection range of 0.1 μM–30 μM for tartrazine. The equation of linear regression was F0/F = 0.9790 + 0.0672C (C is the concentration of tartrazine, μM) with a correlation coefficient 0.9936, and the detection limit was estimated to be 48 nM (The signal-to-noise ratio is 3).

Table 1 presented a comparison of other methods with ours for detecting tartrazine and showed that ours can be economical, simple, rapid and sensitive. Meanwhile, to verify the selectivity of this sensing system, we measured the fluorescence response of N,Cl-FCDs with 18 substances, which might be present in beverages such as K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, HCO3, Cl−, glucose, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, citric acid, lactose, boric acid, rhodamine B, methylene blue, malachite green, sunset yellow, bright blue. The concentration of tartrazine was 20 μM, Rhodamine B, methylene blue, malachite green was 10 times the amount of tartrazine, sunset yellow, bright blue was 1 times the amount of tartrazine. The other interfering substances were 100 times the amount of tartrazine. The results, shown in Fig. 4B, displayed that the analysis strategy designed has high selectivity toward tartrazine detection.

In order to investigate the feasibility of this method, the analysis and detection of tartrazine in beverages was carried out. Beverages were spiked with standard solutions with different concentrations of tartrazine and measured by the proposed method. Table 2 showed that the recoveries were achieved in the range of 99.7%–100.5%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) was between 2.0%–4.0%, which indicated the high analytical precision of the proposed method. All observations further approved the developed N,Cl-FCDs based luminescent probe method was reliable and feasible for the determination of tartrazine in beverages.

In summary, doping is a simple and fast way to tune the fluorescence properties of CDs. The fluorescence properties of N,Cl-FCDs synthesized with the hydrothermal method were greatly improved by N,Cl co-doping, which made them possible to be designed as a fluorescent probe because of their salt and pH tolerance. According to the fluorescence quenching caused by the FRET interaction between tartrazine and N,Cl-FCDs, a rapid selective analysis approach for the determination of tartrazine in beverage was established with detection limit as low as 48 nM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777130, 21277109), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province of China (19YYJC2801), the Meritocracy Research Funds of China West Normal University (463132) and the Fundamental Research Funds of China West Normal University (416390).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.125832.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
